content.text:
{The boundary lines have fallen for me in pleasant places; surely I have a delightful inheritance.}{Psalm 16:6}
 {Algebra is generous; she often gives more than is asked of her.}{— Jean le Rond d'Alembert}

We have seen that a linear system can have zero, one, or infinitely many 
solutions. Now we examine the *structure* of these solution sets, 
discovering that infinite solution sets have beautiful geometric forms.

content.title: Homogeneous System
content.content:
A system of linear equations is called **homogeneous** 
if it has the form $A\vect{x} = \vect{0}$, where $\vect{0}$ is the zero 
vector. Otherwise, the system is **nonhomogeneous**.

content.text:
Every homogeneous system has at least one solution: the **trivial solution** 
$\vect{x} = \vect{0}$. The interesting question is whether *nontrivial* 
solutions exist.

content.title: Nontrivial Solutions
content.content:
The homogeneous system $A\vect{x} = \vect{0}$ has a nontrivial solution 
if and only if the system has at least one free variable.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If the system has a free variable, we can set it to any nonzero value 
and solve for the basic variables, producing a nontrivial solution. 
Conversely, if there are no free variables, every variable is basic, 
and the unique solution is $\vect{x} = \vect{0}$.

content.title: Solving a homogeneous system
content.problem:
Find all solutions to:
\[
\systeme{
x_1 + 3x_2 - 5x_3 = 0,
x_1 + 4x_2 - 8x_3 = 0,
-3x_1 - 7x_2 + 9x_3 = 0
}
\]
content.solution:
Row reduce the coefficient matrix:
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 3 & -5 \\
1 & 4 & -8 \\
-3 & -7 & 9
\end{bmatrix}
\to
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & 4 \\
0 & 1 & -3 \\
0 & 0 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\]
The variable $x_3$ is free. Setting $x_3 = t$:
\[
\vect{x} = \begin{bmatrix} -4t \\ 3t \\ t \end{bmatrix} = 
t \begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\]
The solution set is a **line through the origin** in $\R^3$, 
spanned by the vector $\begin{bmatrix} -4 \\ 3 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}$.

content.text:
When a system has infinitely many solutions, we describe them using 
**free variables as parameters**. The result is a *parametric 
vector form* of the solution.

content.title: Parametric solution with two free variables
content.problem:
Describe the solution set of $A\vect{x} = \vect{0}$ where the reduced 
echelon form of $A$ is:
\[
\begin{bmatrix}
1 & 0 & -3 & 0 & 2 \\
0 & 1 & 2 & 0 & -1 \\
0 & 0 & 0 & 1 & 4
\end{bmatrix}
\]
content.solution:
Variables $x_3$ and $x_5$ are free. From the equations:
$$
x_1 &= 3x_3 - 2x_5 \\
x_2 &= -2x_3 + x_5 \\
x_4 &= -4x_5
$$
Setting $x_3 = s$ and $x_5 = t$:
\[
\vect{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 3s - 2t \\ -2s + t \\ s \\ -4t \\ t \end{bmatrix}
= s\begin{bmatrix} 3 \\ -2 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} + 
t\begin{bmatrix} -2 \\ 1 \\ 0 \\ -4 \\ 1 \end{bmatrix}
\]
The solution set is a **plane through the origin** in $\R^5$, 
spanned by two vectors.

content.title: Structure of Homogeneous Solution Sets
content.content:
The solution set of a homogeneous system $A\vect{x} = \vect{0}$ can 
always be written as $\spn\{\vect{v}_1, \ldots, \vect{v}_k\}$ for some 
vectors $\vect{v}_1, \ldots, \vect{v}_k$. Geometrically, this is always 
a subspace passing through the origin.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Solution set of a nonhomogeneous system
content.problem:
Describe the solution set of:
\[
\systeme{
x_1 - 2x_2 + x_3 = 3,
2x_1 - 4x_2 + 3x_3 = 9
}
\]
content.solution:
Row reducing:
\[
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & -2 & 1 & 3 \\
2 & -4 & 3 & 9
\end{array}\right]
\to
\left[\begin{array}{ccc|c}
1 & -2 & 0 & 0 \\
0 & 0 & 1 & 3
\end{array}\right]
\]
With $x_2 = t$ free:
\[
\vect{x} = \begin{bmatrix} 2t \\ t \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}
= \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix} + 
t\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}
\]
This is a **line**, but not through the origin. It passes through 
$\vect{p} = \begin{bmatrix} 0 \\ 0 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}$ and is parallel 
to the direction $\begin{bmatrix} 2 \\ 1 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}$.

content.title: Structure of General Solution
content.content:
Suppose $A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$ is consistent and $\vect{p}$ is one 
particular solution. Then the solution set is:
\[
\{\vect{p} + \vect{v}_h : \vect{v}_h \text{ solves } A\vect{x} = \vect{0}\}
\]
Written in parametric form:
\[
\vect{x} = \vect{p} + t_1\vect{v}_1 + \cdots + t_k\vect{v}_k
\]
where $\vect{v}_1, \ldots, \vect{v}_k$ span the solution set of the 
associated homogeneous system.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
If $A\vect{p} = \vect{b}$ and $A\vect{v}_h = \vect{0}$, then 
$A(\vect{p} + \vect{v}_h) = A\vect{p} + A\vect{v}_h = \vect{b} + \vect{0} = \vect{b}$.
Conversely, if $A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$, then $A(\vect{x} - \vect{p}) = 
A\vect{x} - A\vect{p} = \vect{b} - \vect{b} = \vect{0}$, so 
$\vect{x} - \vect{p}$ is in the homogeneous solution set.

content.text:
The geometric picture is illuminating: the solution set of 
$A\vect{x} = \vect{b}$ is the homogeneous solution set *shifted* 
by any particular solution $\vect{p}$.

marginNotes.text:
And you will know the truth, and the truth 
will set you free.
